purpose: Give the user a structured notebook for researching a topic — sources, extracted claims, follow-up questions, and free-form notes.
primaryUserGoal: Build up an understanding of a topic with traceable sources and a running list of open questions.
whenHermesShouldChoose: User asks to research a topic, explain why/how something works, or dig into a question that will plausibly need multiple sources.
goodFor: Topic research ('why is the sky blue')
Consolidating scattered reading into one view
supports: Tabs: Sources, Key points, Follow-ups, Notes
Grouped lists by source type or theme
idealDataShape: 3-8 sources grouped by type (academic, web, video, book)

populationInstructions.summary: Create 4 tabs: Sources (grouped-list by source type), Key points (grouped-list by theme), Follow-ups (grouped-list by priority or theme), Notes. Keep per-item subtitles short and accurate.
steps: State the research question clearly in the title/summary.
Sources tab: group by type (Academic papers, Web articles, Books, Videos) and show 3-8 sources total.
Key points tab: group claims by theme. Each item has a short title and a 1-sentence subtitle citing the source.
Follow-ups tab: group open questions (e.g., by urgency or sub-topic). Keep each question specific.
Notes tab: free-form for user's own observations.
guardrails: Every claim in Key points must cite which source it came from.
Don't fabricate paper titles or authors.
Keep follow-up questions answerable — avoid vague 'learn more'.

patchPrefer: Use upsert_groups to add a new source or claim as research progresses
Use append_note_lines to capture a quick observation
Use set_active_tab when user switches focus
